text: In this video I'm breaking down the robotic lower anterior resection with total mesorectal excision into twenty one steps.
I promise you that if you follow these steps in this order it will make your operation progress smoothly and move along at a much quicker pace.

These cases for me began in a haphazard manner and I became extremely frustrated that the cases were really long and they really were not satisfying.

So that was until I wrote down all the steps of the operation and made sure that when I began that step of the operation, I actually finished it robotically and then went to the next step. Step one, position patient.

Sounds a little funny that I'll start with this one, but seriously, it's probably one of the more important parts of the case so that you protect the patient and to prevent external collisions, which obviously is a frustrating part of the case.
The surgeon accesses the abdomen and begins insufflation, preparing the surgical field for robotic intervention.
I typically do an optical entry technique, but you can do really whatever you'd like.
Step three, place your ports in whichever port configuration you're comfortable with.
I'll link to a video below about my port configuration in extensive detail for low interior section.
Step four, we're going to explore the abdomen.
The surgeon positions the bowel within the abdominal cavity, adjusting its placement to facilitate further surgical access and visualization.

I can't overstress this enough. Don't even look at the robot until the bowel has been correctly positioned in the upper abdomen and you can easily see the sacral pulmonary and the inferior mesentery artery pedicle before you dock the robot.
Trust me, it's going to save you a headache.
Step six, initiate dissection.

A couple ways to do this, we can start up with a medial to lateral dissection technique versus lateral to medial. Now, for the medial to lateral, there are a couple of approaches. You can go infra-IMA first, you can go supra-IMA first, or you can go sub-IMV first.

Either one of those are acceptable, and we'll talk about what my preference is in a later video. Or you can do the good old lateral to medial, which is how I trained in residency and fellowship. And in
which is actually one of the reasons I started this YouTube channel. So actually, let me ask a question now.
In the comment section, let me know what your preferred technique is, whether it's medial-lateral, lateral-medial, infra-IMA approach, supra-IMA approach, or sub-IMV approach.
The surgeon identifies and preserves the hypogastric nerves, followed by the identification and preservation of the left ureter. Subsequently, the IMA pedicle is skeletonized and divided.
The surgeon identifies, skeletonizes, and divides the inferior mesenteric vein, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon prepares to mobilize the splenic flexure, indicating that this step is performed selectively based on procedural requirements.
For the Sputnik flexural mobilization, there's two ways to do this. Also, a medial approach versus a lateral approach.

The surgeon mobilizes the descending and sigmoid colon down to the level of the parent neural affection. This is followed by circumferential dissection of the rectum, starting posteriorly and extending distally, then moving anteriorly towards the anal canal or pelvic floor. The lateral stalks are dissected last.

Now I know this is a bit different than what most surgeons will do and while there's no wrong way to do this, in a later video I'm gonna cover extensively the reason and the rationale and the execution for this dissection technique.
The surgeon performs a division of the rectum, typically using total mesorectal excision. For more proximally located tumors, a tumor-specific mesorectal excision is employed.
The surgeon selects a proximal division point and proceeds to divide the mesentery at the descending sigmoid junction. The perfusion of the proposed anastomotic site is assessed visually.

Step eighteen, we're going to set up for the colorectal anastomosis, and I'll link to a video that goes into exhaustive detail about the different methods by which this anastomosis can be fashioned, whether it's intracorporeal versus extracorporeal. And if you do choose an intracorporeal, you can do a side-to-side, you can do an end-to-side, you can do an end-to-end. Really, the options are kind of endless.

Then we go to step nineteen, which is hydrodynamic testing of the anastomosis. Step twenty, if appropriate, the patient will get a diverting loop ileostomy. And finally, with step twenty-one, which is closure of the abdomen.
All right, so there you have it. Twenty one steps to a robotic low interior section with TME.
